event: The Creation of God — reimagined Creation of Adam
symbols: fingers nearly touching — the divine spark of life passing from Creator to creature
God depicted as a Black woman — reimagining the divine image to affirm the Imago Dei in all humanity
billowing cloak with attendant figures — the heavenly court surrounding the Creator
reclining figure on earth — the newly created human receiving the gift of life
description: A reimagining of Michelangelo's Creation of Adam with Black figures. At the left, a reclining female figure lies on golden-brown earth, one arm extended. From the right, a figure surrounded by attendants floats within a billowing pink cloak, reaching out with one hand toward the reclining figure. Their fingers nearly touch across the gap. The attendant figures within the cloak include adults and children. Clouds fill the background. The composition directly mirrors the original Sistine Chapel fresco but with dark-skinned figures throughout.
theological_description: This bold reinterpretation of the Creation affirms the biblical truth that all human beings are created in God's image (Genesis 1:27) — a truth often obscured by centuries of racially exclusive artistic tradition. The iconic gesture of the nearly-touching fingers — the moment of divine life being transmitted to the creature — transcends any particular ethnicity. By depicting the Creator and creature as Black women, the artist recovers the universality of the Imago Dei and challenges the viewer to see divinity reflected in every face. Genesis declares that God created humanity — male and female — in the divine image, without specifying appearance. The painting thus serves as visual exegesis: the image of God is not confined to one race or gender but is the birthright of all. This is the theological foundation for human dignity that both the prophets (Malachi 2:10) and the apostles (Acts 17:26) affirmed.

scripture_refs.reason: God created humanity in His own image, male and female — the Imago Dei affirmed in all people
The Lord God formed man and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life — the moment of creation depicted
From one man He made every nation — the unity of humanity in God's creation
Have we not all one Father? Has not one God created us? — the universal fatherhood of God
